---
title: "Exercise2_larose"
author: "Sam Scott"
date: "2026-03-03"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

bank <- read.csv('C:/Users/samsc/Desktop/ADS-502-Hands-On-Exercises/ADS-Hands-On-Exercises/bank.csv', sep = ';')

#creating contingency table
contin_table <- table(bank$previous, bank$y)
contin_table

#get the first 9 records of the bank dataframe and save it as its own df
first_nine = bank[c(1:9), ]
first_nine

#get only age and marital status from the bank dataframe and save as its own df
age_marital = bank[c(1,3)]
age_marital

#get the first three rows of the age_martial df and save as its own df
first_three_am = age_marital[c(1:3),]
first_three_am


#Indexing in R and Python are quite different but the ideas are the same
